headline: What's Coming
subhead: Two projections based on current trajectory

projections.headline: Credit Bureaus Will Be #1 by Late 2025
projections.projection_methodology: Linear extrapolation of 2022-2024 growth rates. Credit Bureaus growing at 51.3% CAGR, Identity Theft at 1.27% CAGR. At current rates, Credit Bureaus overtakes Identity Theft in late 2025.

copy.short: At current growth rates, Credit Bureau complaints will surpass Identity Theft as the #1 category by late 2025.
copy.body: Credit Bureau complaints have grown from 590,872 (2022) to 1,353,175 (2024), a 129% increase. Identity Theft has grown just 2.56% over the same period. If these trends continue, Credit Bureaus will become the dominant complaint category within 12-18 months. This signals a structural shift: fraud's aftermath, not fraud itself, is becoming the dominant consumer complaint.

projections.what_it_means: The fraud has already happened. Consumers are now fighting to clean up the damage. This is a lagging indicator of identity theft that occurred months or years earlier.

projections.headline: Expect Regulatory Pressure on Social Media Platforms

regulatory_signals: FCC considering caller ID authentication requirements
SCAM Act in Congress targeting social media fraud
78% of consumers support legislation requiring social media companies to remove fraudulent ads
FTC increasing enforcement on platform-enabled fraud

copy.short: Social media's 7x higher conversion rate will attract regulatory attention.
copy.body: With $1.86B in losses and a 70% conversion rate, social media has become the preferred channel for sophisticated scammers. Regulatory bodies are taking notice. The FCC is considering stricter caller ID authentication, and the SCAM Act in Congress would require social media companies to combat fraud more aggressively. If social media-initiated fraud continues growing, expect platform liability to become a major policy battleground.

projections.what_it_means: Fraud teams should monitor regulatory developments. New platform requirements could shift fraud patterns to other contact channels.

caveats: Projections assume continuation of 2022-2024 trends. Regulatory action, economic changes, or platform policy shifts could alter trajectory.
Credit Bureau complaint growth may plateau as identity theft cleanup backlogs clear.
Social media platform changes (e.g., account verification requirements) could reduce fraud conversion rates.
